title: How variety helps ecosystems absorb shocks

lesson.learn:
Ecological diversity works like a kind of backup system. When many species fill similar roles, an ecosystem is less likely to fail if one species declines. Different species also respond to drought, heat, pests, and disturbance in different ways, which helps the whole system keep functioning.

That does not mean diversity prevents every problem. It means the system has more options. The more functional diversity an ecosystem has, the better it can spread risk, recover after stress, and avoid sudden collapse.
lesson.simpler: Ecosystems with more kinds of living things are usually tougher. If one species struggles, another can sometimes fill its role.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Diversity improves stability because it gives ecosystems more ways to keep working after a shock.
lesson.deeperDive:
Ecologists often talk about redundancy, response diversity, and the portfolio effect. Redundancy means several species can perform a similar function. Response diversity means those species do not all react the same way to stress. The portfolio effect is the same idea people use in finance: spreading risk makes the whole system less fragile.

That is why biodiversity is not just about counting species. It is about building a system with enough variation to keep energy, nutrients, and interactions moving even when the environment changes.
lesson.funFact: In ecology, variety is a form of insurance.

places: Species-rich grasslands - often resilient to change
Coral reefs - diversity can buffer local stress
Tropical forests - many niches help stability
years: 1950s - stability becomes a big ecology question
2020s - response diversity is a major theme
concepts: Functional diversity - different jobs in the ecosystem
Response diversity - different reactions to the same stress
Redundancy - more than one species can do the job
